content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 958 I 4-2329 
Steven T. Mattas 
Attorney at Law 
575 Market Street, Suite 2080 
San Francisco, CA 94105 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-13-098 
Dear Mr. Mattas: 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
July 22, 2013 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City Councilmember Mark 
Addiego regarding the conllict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that because the Fair Poiitical Practices Commbsion ("the Commission") does not 
act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), we base 
this letter on the facts presented. 
QUESTION 
Does Council member Addiego have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act? 
CONCLUSION 
The governmental decision Mr. Addiego's agency will make will not have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on his employer. Therefore, he is not prohibited from 
participating in the decision. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references arc to the Gmernmenl Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references arc to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. 1-13-098 
Page No. 2 
You are the City Attorney for the City of South San Francisco and you seek advice on 
behalf of South San Francisco City Council Member Mark Addiego. Mr. Addiego works as the 
District Operations Manager for the Cemetery Department of the Archdiocese of San Francisco. 
The city's planning commission approved a conditional use permit granted to Planned 
Parenthood for a medical clinic. A group called "Respect Life South San Francisco" appealed 
that decision, and that appeal is now before the city council 
The Archdiocese is composed of a number of separate operating divisions, one of which 
is the division that runs the cemeteries. You recently received information from the Archdiocese 
that it is not monetarily affiliated with Respect Life San Francisco, though it does offer spiritual 
support and prayers. The Archdiocese of San Francisco also informed you that its gross receipts 
are approximately $140,000,000. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within 
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; 
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding 
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. 
You have explained that steps one and two of the analysis are met: Mr. Addiego is a 
public official and will be called upon to participate in the appeal described above. 
Step Three: What are Mr. Addigo's interests? 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of section 
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated interests. Among these interests is any 
source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior 
to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Reg. 18703.3.). Mr. Addiego therefore has a source of 
income interest in the cemetery division of the Archdiocese. 
You have not offered any information regarding any other interests. 


File No. 1-13-098 
Page No. 3 
Step Four: Is Mr. Addiego's employer directly or indirectly related to the governmental 
decisions? 
A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, is directly 
involved in a decision before an agency when that person, either directly or by an agent: 
"( 1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing 
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the 
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a 
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or 
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
subject person." (Regulation 18704.1 (a).) 
"Respect Life South San Francisco" has filed the appeal of the planning commission's 
decision. As stated above, Mr. Addiego's interest is in the Archdiocese of San Francisco, which 
has no financial connection to Respect Life South San Francisco. Mr. Addiego's interest has not, 
therefore, initiated the proceeding, nor is it the subject of a claim. His interest is indirectly 
involved in the governmental decisions. 
Steps Five and Six: What is the applicable materiality standard and is it reasonably 
foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental decision upon the officials' interest 
will meet this materiality standard? 
The effect on a source of income that is indirectly involved in a decision is subject to the 
materiality standards in Regulation 18705.1 (c). The information you provided states that the 
Archdiocese of San Francisco would fall under Regulation 18705. l (c)(3), having an income of 
"not less than $750,000. To be material, the decision would have to result in an increase or 
decrease to the business entity's gross income by $300,000 or more or it would result in an 
increase or decrease in the business's assets or liabilities by $100,000 or more. 
The Archdiocese has no financial involvement with Respect Life South San Francisco, 
which is the entity that filed the appeal before the city council. Accordingly, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable that there will be any financial effect on the Archdiocese of San Francisco based on 
your facts. 


File No. 1-13-098 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HMR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
By: 
Heather M. Rowan 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

The governmental decision Mr. Addiego's agency will make will not have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on his employer. Therefore, he is not prohibited from 
participating in the decision. 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission arc contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations.
